title: How herbal infusions work
subtitle: The extraction science behind the covered cup
excerpt: What actually happens when you steep herbs in hot water, why the lid matters, what the steep time does, why some plants need cold water instead of hot, and how to read a recipe that says 'infusion' versus 'decoction'. The practical foundations for every hot herb preparation.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), general introduction, infusion and decoction methods described. Traditional apothecary practice as documented across European pharmacopoeia texts. EMA HMPC guidance on herbal preparations, definitions of infusion, decoction, cold maceration.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation made by pouring hot water over plant material and steeping, covered, for a set time. Used for flowers, leaves, and delicate parts where heat would destroy the actives.
A preparation made by simmering plant material in water for 20, 40 minutes. Used for tougher plant parts, roots, bark, berries, that require sustained heat to release their constituents.
A preparation made by steeping plant material in cold water for several hours. Used for plants, such as marshmallow root, whose active constituents (mucilage) are destroyed by heat.
The aromatic fraction of a herb that evaporates readily with heat. The reason the lid is essential in an infusion: without it, the volatile oils escape with the steam before you can drink them.

content.text: An
infusion

is what most people call a herb tea. Hot water poured over plant material, covered, and left to steep. Simple, but the details matter more than most people realise. This article explains the mechanics behind the covered cup, the steep time, and the choice between hot and cold water, so that every infusion recipe in this section makes sense rather than just being a set of steps to follow.
Why the lid is not optional
Every infusion recipe in this section says to put the lid on immediately. This is the single most important instruction in herbal infusion preparation and the most commonly ignored. The reason is
volatile oils

, the aromatic fraction of the herb that carries much of the medicinal action. Volatile oils are, by definition, volatile: they evaporate at near-boiling temperatures and escape with the steam. An uncovered cup of chamomile tea loses its antispasmodic bisabolol into the kitchen air within the first minute of steeping. The cup that arrives at the table smells of chamomile but has lost most of its medicinal value. The lid keeps the volatile oils in. A saucer over the cup does the same job if you don't have a lid.
Why steep time matters

The standard steep time for most herbal infusions is 10 minutes. This is long enough to extract the primary active constituents from flowers and leaves, and short enough to avoid over-extracting the tannins that make the cup bitter and harsh. Under-steeping (two to three minutes) produces a pleasant-tasting, weakly therapeutic cup, the volatile oils have started to infuse, but the slower-releasing polyphenols and flavonoids are still mostly in the plant material. Over-steeping (more than 15 minutes) begins to extract more tannins, making the cup more astringent and bitter without significantly increasing the active constituent content.
Hot infusion versus decoction

Flowers and leaves, chamomile, lavender, lemon balm, elderflower, nettle, peppermint, are delicate enough to release their constituents into hot water without requiring sustained heat. A 10-minute infusion works. Roots, bark, seeds, and berries, valerian root, elderberries, fennel seeds, are denser and tougher. Their cell walls do not break open at steeping temperature. A
decoction

, sustained simmering in a covered pan for 20 to 40 minutes, is required. Fennel seeds are the middle case: lightly crushing them before infusing breaks the seed coat and allows hot-water infusion to work without full simmering. This is why the fennel tea recipe specifically calls for crushing the seeds first.
When cold water works better than hot

Marshmallow root is the exception that proves the rule. Its primary therapeutic compound is mucilage, a polysaccharide gel that swells in water to coat and soothe mucous membranes. Heat destroys the mucilage. A hot infusion of marshmallow root produces a thinner, more bitter liquid than a
cold infusion

, the cold water slowly draws out the mucilage over eight to twelve hours without destroying it. If a recipe in this section specifies cold water and overnight steeping, this is the reason: the active constituent requires cold extraction.
Water temperature

The standard instruction is 'just off the boil', water that has been boiled and then allowed to stand for 30 to 60 seconds, reaching approximately 90, 95°C. This is hot enough to extract effectively but not so aggressively hot that it drives volatile oils out of the plant material instantly on contact. Boiling water poured directly from a rolling boil is slightly hotter than ideal for delicate flowers; it makes very little practical difference, but the 30-second wait is not a ritual, it has a small practical effect.
What 'infusion' means on a label

The term 'infusion' in a recipe heading means: pour hot water over, cover, steep 10 minutes, strain. It does not mean simmer, boil, or cook. 'Decoction' means simmer covered. 'Cold infusion' or 'cold maceration' means cold water, no heat, steep overnight. These three methods are not interchangeable, using the wrong one produces either a weaker or a more bitter preparation than intended, and for marshmallow root, it produces a preparation that lacks its primary therapeutic compound entirely.
